- "No real roots" is interpreted as P(x)\neq0 for all real x; for monic quartics this is equivalent to strict positivity.

Let W be the set of real polynomials P of degree at most 4 satisfying the symmetry
P(2-x)=P(x) for all real x.

Count the number of monic quartics
P(x)=x^4+a_3x^3+a_2x^2+a_1x+a_0
with integers a_0,a_1,a_2,a_3 satisfying |a_k|\leq7 for k=0,1,2,3, such that P\inW and P has no real zeros.

Solution.
Step 1 (Technique: center_shift_to_even_polynomial; Operators: translation_substitution, even_polynomial_characterization)
The condition P(2-x)=P(x) means P is symmetric about x=1. Let y=x-1 and define Q(y)=P(y+1).
Then for all y,
Q(-y)=P(1-y)=P(1+y)=Q(y),
so Q is an even polynomial. Since P is monic quartic, Q is a monic even quartic, hence must have the form
Q(y)=y^4 + b y^2 + d
for some real b,d.

Equivalently,
P(x)=Q(x-1)=(x-1)^4 + b(x-1)^2 + d.

Step 2 (Technique: coefficient_comparison_and_parameterization; Operator: coefficient_comparison)
Expand:
(x-1)^4 = x^4 -4x^3 +6x^2 -4x +1,
b(x-1)^2 = b(x^2-2x+1)=b x^2 -2b x + b.
Thus
P(x)=x^4 + (-4)x^3 + (6+b)x^2 + (-4-2b)x + (1+b+d).
So the coefficient relations forced by symmetry are
a_3 = -4,
a_2 = 6 + b,
a_1 = -4 - 2b,
a_0 = 1 + b + d,
with b,d integers whenever a_0,a_1,a_2,a_3 are integers (indeed b=a_2-6 and d=a_0-a_2+5).

Step 3 (Technique: translate_bounds_to_integer_constraints; Operator: inequality_solving_over_integers)
Impose the coefficient bounds |a_k|\leq7.

From a_3=-4 we have |a_3|=4\leq7, always satisfied.

From |a_2|\leq7:
|6+b|\leq7  ⇔  -7\leq6+b\leq7  ⇔  -13\leqb\leq1.

From |a_1|\leq7 and a_1=-4-2b:
|-4-2b|\leq7  ⇔  -7\leq-4-2b\leq7.
The left inequality gives -3\leq-2b, i.e. 2b\leq3, hence b\leq1.
The right inequality gives -2b\leq11, hence b\geq-11/2, i.e. b\geq-5.
So b must satisfy -5\leqb\leq1.

Therefore
b \in {-5,-4,-3,-2,-1,0,1}.

Finally, from |a_0|\leq7 and a_0=1+b+d:
-7 \leq 1+b+d \leq 7  ⇔  -8-b \leq d \leq 6-b.
So for each fixed b, d ranges over the integers in [ -8-b, 6-b ].

Step 4 (Technique: reduce_quartic_positivity_to_quadratic_on_z_ge_0; Operators: variable_change_z_equals_y_squared, quadratic_minimization_on_halfline)
Since P is monic quartic, P(x)\to+∞ as x\to±∞. Therefore "no real zeros" is equivalent to strict positivity:
P(x)>0 for all real x.
Because P(x)=Q(x-1), this is equivalent to
Q(y)=y^4 + b y^2 + d > 0 for all real y.

Let z=y^2, so z\geq0. Then
Q(y)=z^2 + b z + d =: f(z),
and we need
f(z)>0 for all z\geq0.

Step 5 (Technique: vertex_casework_for_quadratic_on_halfline; Theorem: quadratic_minimum_at_vertex)
For f(z)=z^2+bz+d on z\geq0, consider the sign of b:

- If b\geq0, then f′(z)=2z+b>0 for all z\geq0, so f is increasing on [0,∞). The minimum occurs at z=0, hence
f(z)>0 for all z\geq0  ⇔  f(0)=d>0.

- If b<0, the vertex occurs at z=-b/2>0, so the minimum on [0,∞) occurs at the vertex. The minimum value is
f(-b/2)=d - b^2/4.
Thus
f(z)>0 for all z\geq0  ⇔  d - b^2/4 > 0  ⇔  d > b^2/4.

Step 6 (Technique: integer_threshold_counting; Operator: finite_enumeration)
Now count integer pairs (b,d) with b\in{-5,-4,-3,-2,-1,0,1}, d in the allowed interval, and the positivity condition.

For each b, the allowed d-range is d \in [ -8-b, 6-b ].

Case b=0 (b\geq0): d\in[-8,6] and need d>0, so d=1,2,3,4,5,6: 6 choices.

Case b=1 (b\geq0): d\in[-9,5] and need d>0, so d=1,2,3,4,5: 5 choices.

Case b=-1: need d>1/4, so d\geq1. Range d\in[-7,7], so d=1,2,3,4,5,6,7: 7 choices.

Case b=-2: need d>4/4=1, so d\geq2. Range d\in[-6,8], so d=2,3,4,5,6,7,8: 7 choices.

Case b=-3: need d>9/4=2.25, so d\geq3. Range d\in[-5,9], so d=3,4,5,6,7,8,9: 7 choices.

Case b=-4: need d>16/4=4, so d\geq5. Range d\in[-4,10], so d=5,6,7,8,9,10: 6 choices.

Case b=-5: need d>25/4=6.25, so d\geq7. Range d\in[-3,11], so d=7,8,9,10,11: 5 choices.

Total:
6 + 5 + 7 + 7 + 7 + 6 + 5 = 43.

Therefore the number of such polynomials is 43.

Answer: 43.

Uniqueness Test
(Referencing the uniqueness_certificate(s) in the New Metadata: the symmetry forces a unique (b,d)-parameterization; positivity reduces to a unique set of strict inequalities; the finite count of integer solutions is therefore uniquely determined.)

1) Symmetry P(2-x)=P(x) forces Q(y)=P(y+1) to be even, hence Q(y)=y^4+by^2+d with uniquely determined real numbers b,d. Conversely, each choice of (b,d) produces exactly one P via P(x)=Q(x-1). Thus each admissible polynomial corresponds to exactly one pair (b,d).

2) The integer coefficient requirement implies b and d are integers (since b=a_2-6 and d=a_0-a_2+5), and the coefficient bounds |a_k|\leq7 translate into the explicit finite constraints b\in{-5,-4,-3,-2,-1,0,1} and d\in[-8-b,6-b]. There is no ambiguity in this translation.

3) For a monic quartic, "no real zeros" is equivalent to strict positivity everywhere. Under the substitution z=y^2\geq0, strict positivity is equivalent to f(z)=z^2+bz+d>0 for all z\geq0, whose necessary-and-sufficient conditions are uniquely:
- if b\geq0 then d>0,
- if b<0 then d>b^2/4.
These conditions are forced by the unique minimum of a quadratic on a half-line.

4) Therefore the admissible set is exactly the set of integer pairs (b,d) satisfying the stated inequalities. Counting these pairs yields a single integer (43), so the answer is unique.

43
